steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank falls at the end of a relative clause ("which ceased to be part of the empire in [year]") and before an independent clause ("the latter's connection to the empire is so attenuated that Spanish is seldom spoken there today"). The second clause elaborates on the consequence of the Netherlands leaving the empire. Choose the punctuation that correctly connects these two clauses.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 1648,: A comma alone cannot join two independent clauses — this creates a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice B — 1648: No punctuation between two independent clauses is a run-on sentence. ✗
  • Choice C — 1648:: A colon correctly introduces an elaboration or consequence of the preceding statement. The second clause explains how weak the Netherlands' connection to the empire has become — exactly what a colon signals. ✓
  • Choice D — 1648 and: "And" would need a comma before it to join two independent clauses, and even then it would blur the cause-and-effect relationship that a colon makes clear. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
